You know what 59 Redditors just confirmed?

Something I've been dealing with for weeks.
LLMs losing instructions mid-conversation.
And it's the same problem as how
ADHD brains lose focus.
Not a metaphor. Actually the same mechanism.

Here's what's going on.

LLMs decide what matters through "attention."
The longer your conversation gets,
the more your original instructions get diluted.
Like, completely gone.

I used to set up a task with all the constraints.
And 12 messages later Claude is doing
something entirely different.
I thought I was just bad at prompting lol

But once I came to know about
the actual mechanism, fixing it was pretty simple.

I now restate my core instructions
every 5-8 messages.
Just one line. Takes two seconds.
It re-injects the destination
so the model doesn't wander.

I also do context checkpoints.
Every 10-15 messages I ask Claude
to summarize progress and restate what's left.
Then I use that summary as the new starting point.
To be very honest,
this one made the biggest difference.

And the last thing.
Structured prompts over conversational ones.
Role. Task. Constraints. Output format.
The trick is very simple,
but people complicate it.
That structure alone resists attention decay.

My sessions now last 30-40 plus messages
without losing the plot.
You just have to work with how
attention actually functions.

Why keep it to myself?
I thought I could share this small value
with you all as well.

I created a Long-Context Prompt Cheat Sheet.
Copy-paste templates for all three techniques.

The 15-minute Video Sales Letter is dying. And it is taking your conversion rate down with it.

We used to see 3-8% conversion rates with standard VSL funnels. Those days are over.

In 2025, asking a cold prospect to sit through a linear 12-minute video without the ability to skip ahead is asking for too much trust.

We audited our drop-off rates recently. We found users were leaving at the 30-second mark. They never even heard the offer.

Here is the reality:

1. Most mobile users browse with sound off. If your pitch relies on audio, you are pitching to a deaf room.
2. Heavy video loads slower. Every second of delay costs you conversions.
3. Attention spans are fragmented. Users decide in seconds if a page is worth their time.

We shifted our strategy to what I call "Low-Friction" text sales pages.

This is not a wall of text. It is a structured argument.

It allows the user to be in control. They can scroll, scan headers, read the bullets, and get the gist in under 30 seconds.

When we tested this against traditional VSLs on cold traffic, the text pages matched and often beat the video versions.

Clarity converts better than cleverness.

If you are selling a $10,000 coaching program, you might still need video for emotion. But for most offers, speed is the new currency.

Stop forcing your customers to watch at your pace. Let them buy at theirs.

Creative design and variation are now the single biggest levers for lowering costs. The algorithm rewards engaging content with cheaper delivery.

Stop telling Facebook who to target. Start showing them.

You just generated 1,000 fresh leads from a campaign.

You feed them into your automation to update the CRM.

Then the scenario crashes.

You are stuck manually fixing data or losing potential revenue in the digital void.

Here is the hard truth: Your logic isn't broken. Your volume handling is.

Most automation platforms have safeguards to stop one user from hogging resources. When you try to push 1,000 records through a single module at once, you trip the wires.

The solution isn't a more expensive plan.

It is using the Iterator module.

Think of your data like a stack of 1,000 physical forms. Without an Iterator, your automation tries to stamp the whole stack at once. Naturally, it drops them.

The Iterator picks up one form, stamps it, files it, and moves to the next.

But even with an Iterator, you can still fail if you ignore Rate Limits.

If you send requests too fast, destination apps like HubSpot or OpenAI will block you.

The fix?

Insert a "Sleep" module after your action step inside the loop. Set it to 2 seconds.

This forces a tiny pause between each lead.

It turns a crash-prone workflow into a system that scales from 10 leads to 10,000 without you touching a button.
